text: This fifty-five years old male presented with complaint of pain, recurrent pain in epigastric and right hypochondriac region. And from last two days before he presented to me, he was having vomiting and fever.
and upon examination the Murphy's sign was positive so there was the history of total three days of the acute attack
The laparoscopic view reveals significant adhesions and thickening of the gallbladder wall, indicating inflammation.
The surgeon uses a combination of electrocautery and a Maryland dissector to perform both blunt and sharp dissection on the gallbladder, navigating through areas of adherence and thickened tissue.
The surgeon encounters adhesions around the gallbladder and uses electrocautery and blunt dissection techniques, along with hydro dissection, to carefully separate the tissues.
The surgeon grasps the gallbladder proximally and pulls it upwards to enhance visibility and clarify the anatomy in the gallbladder neck region.
So whenever you feel the need, give more traction towards the right shoulder.
The surgeon applies tension to the gallbladder neck, enhancing the visibility of anatomical structures in the Calot's triangle region.
There would be less risk of injury to the CBD with the help of traction, but only when doing the adrenolysis.
The surgeon relaxes the gallbladder while ligating or clipping the cystic duct to prevent injury to the common bile duct (CBD).

The surgeon monitors the Rubio sulcus to determine the limit of dissection, ensuring that the dissection remains above this anatomical landmark, particularly during the ligation or clipping of the cystic duct.
Dense adhesions are visible around the gallbladder neck and within the Calot's triangle region, complicating the dissection process.

because of two to three reasons. One is that there are the loss of true anatomical planes. You can cause the injury to any vital structure. And secondly, there is increased vascularity due to the ADNs. So there is also a risk of bleeding.
The surgeon performs hydro dissection using a 5mm sucker, applying a flow of water to clearly delineate anatomical planes while simultaneously conducting adrenolysis.
The surgeon performs gradual adenolysis around the junction of the cystic duct and gallbladder neck, ensuring any small oozing does not obstruct the view.
The surgeon uses the tip of a sucker to create a plane between the cystic artery and the dilated cystic duct, carefully separating these structures.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to address small bends in the tissue planes around the gallbladder neck and Calot's triangle, which are not amenable to blunt dissection.
The surgeon lifts the gallbladder neck above the sylovial sulcus following degenolysis, enhancing the clarity of the anatomical structures in the surgical field.

The surgeon exposes Hartman's pouch with clear visibility, performing additional adenolysis in the region. Below Hartman's pouch, the cystic duct is visible, and the surgeon further scalpelizes the duct, noting an injury to the cystic duct.

The surgeon uses the tip of a hook in direct contact with the cystic duct to improve visibility. The cystic artery is clipped, and the distal part of the artery is coagulated to assess the extent of the cystic duct injury.
The surgeon uses the tip of the hook to assess the extent of the iatrogenic injury to the cystic duct, identifying a pinpoint injury.

The surgeon performs hydro dissection in the middle part of the cystic duct, using a hook to explore a suspicious structure near the gallbladder fossa. Further dissection is done to clarify the view, including cyclinitization of the cystic duct. The surgeon reflects the peritoneum on the right side of the cystic duct and uses Maryland forceps and blunt dissection to create space in the groove of this reflection.
The surgeon exposes the proximal length of the cystic duct, providing a clear view of its anatomical structure.

The surgeon performs gentle adhesiolysis to reveal a structure lying anteriorly over the common bile duct (CBD). The view clarifies a pinpoint iatrogenic injury in the middle of the cystic duct, alongside a pulsating artery extending into the gallbladder and liver bed.
The surgeon observes a structure with pulsations and two branches, indicating variable anatomy, as viewed from the right side of the surgical field.
The surgeon dissects the peritoneal attachments on both medial and lateral aspects of the gallbladder, aiming to clarify the view and anatomical planes around a variable artery.
The surgeon plans to apply a clip over the more prominent right-sided branch of the vessel, which is visible and has taken its course deep into the liver parenchyma.

The surgeon uses hydro dissection to skeletonize a vessel, applying clips both proximally and distally before cutting between them. The lumen of the vessel is visible. Clips are then applied to the cystic duct, with the first clip near the junction of the CBD and the cystic duct, and the second clip proximal to the iatrogenic injury.

The surgeon ensures a minimum distance of two millimeters between the clips placed on the cystic duct, with the last clip positioned near the gallbladder neck. The cystic duct is cut between the second and third clips at the site of the previous iatrogenic injury, which is located towards the right side. Hydro dissection is suggested for separating the gallbladder from the liver bed.
The surgeon uses electrocautery gently and meticulously to dissect the gallbladder from the liver bed, combining techniques to smoothly separate the tissue planes once identified.

The surgeon uses electrocautery in spray mode with intermittent pulsatile current to minimize smoke production, enhancing visibility of the dissection plane. The gallbladder is gradually freed from its final attachment to the liver bed near the fundus, completing the dissection.

The surgeon uses a grasper to remove the gallbladder through the epigastric port, with an option to use the umbilical port for cosmetic reasons. A drain is placed inside the surgical site for postoperative fluid management.
